name: Distributed Cache
overview:
A distributed cache stores frequently accessed data in memory across a cluster of nodes to reduce database
load and serve requests with sub-millisecond latency. The system must support horizontal scaling (add/remove
nodes without downtime), handle node failures gracefully, provide configurable eviction policies (LRU, LFU,
TTL), and maintain consistency guarantees appropriate for the use case. The cache must handle millions of
operations per second across thousands of clients.

inScope: Horizontal scaling: add/remove nodes with minimal disruption
LRU eviction when memory is full
Replication for fault tolerance (1 primary + 2 replicas)

items: Support 1M ops/sec across the cluster with <1ms p99 latency
Horizontal scaling: add/remove nodes with minimal key redistribution (consistent hashing)
Fault tolerant: node failure triggers automatic replica promotion within 10 seconds
Eventual consistency for replica reads; strong consistency available for write-through mode
Memory-efficient: slab allocator prevents fragmentation; eviction protects against OOM
Zero-downtime topology changes: clients update routing via gossip without restart

entities.name: Cache Entry
entities.schema:
{
  key: string (max 256 bytes),
  value: bytes (max 1MB),
  ttl: seconds (0 = no expiry),
  created_at: timestamp,
  last_accessed: timestamp,
  access_count: int
}

entities.name: Node
entities.schema:
{
  node_id: string,
  host: string,
  port: int,
  status: enum(PRIMARY|REPLICA|JOINING|LEAVING),
  slot_range: [start, end],  // consistent hash slots
  memory_used: bytes,
  memory_total: bytes
}

endpoints.response:
{ key, value (base64), ttl_remaining }
Response 404: { error: 'key not found' }

{ key, deleted: true }
Response 404: { error: 'key not found' }

approaches.title: Modulo Hashing
approaches.description: Assign key to node using hash(key) % N where N is the number of nodes. Simple but adding or removing a node requires rehashing almost all keys (N-1)/N of all keys move). This causes a thundering herd on the database as the cache is effectively invalidated.
approaches.tradeoff: Adding/removing nodes causes massive cache invalidation; not suitable for dynamic clusters.

approaches.title: Consistent Hashing (Basic)
approaches.description: Map both keys and nodes to a hash ring (0 to 2^32). A key is assigned to the first node clockwise on the ring. Adding a node only moves ~1/N of keys. Removing a node only affects keys that were assigned to it. Basic consistent hashing can cause uneven distribution if nodes are not evenly spaced.
approaches.tradeoff: Uneven load distribution without virtual nodes; hot spots possible.

approaches.title: Consistent Hashing with Virtual Nodes
approaches.description: Each physical node is represented by 150 virtual nodes on the hash ring. This distributes load evenly even with heterogeneous node sizes. When a node joins, it takes over a proportional share of slots from its neighbors. When it leaves, its slots are distributed to neighbors. The cluster coordinator (using Raft consensus) maintains the ring topology and propagates changes to all clients via gossip protocol.
approaches.tradeoff: More complex ring management; gossip protocol adds network overhead.

approaches.title: Simple TTL-Only Eviction
approaches.description: Only evict keys when their TTL expires. No proactive eviction when memory is full. Memory can fill up if TTLs are long or keys have no TTL. Cache becomes unavailable when OOM.
approaches.tradeoff: OOM risk; no protection against memory exhaustion.

approaches.title: LRU with Doubly-Linked List + HashMap
approaches.description: Maintain a doubly-linked list ordered by access time. HashMap provides O(1) key lookup. On access, move the node to the head. On eviction, remove from the tail. Classic LRU implementation. Works well but the linked list has poor cache locality and high memory overhead per entry.
approaches.tradeoff: High memory overhead per entry; poor CPU cache performance due to pointer chasing.

approaches.title: Segmented LRU (SLRU) with Clock Algorithm
approaches.description: Divide cache into protected (80%) and probationary (20%) segments. New entries go to probationary. On second access, promote to protected. Eviction targets probationary segment first. The Clock algorithm approximates LRU with O(1) operations and better cache locality than linked list. Combined with TTL-based lazy expiration (check TTL on access) and periodic active expiration (scan 20 random keys every 100ms, delete expired ones).
approaches.tradeoff: More complex implementation; SLRU requires tuning segment sizes.

components.name: Cache Nodes
components.responsibility: In-memory key-value store; each node owns a range of hash slots; primary + 2 replicas

components.name: Cluster Coordinator
components.responsibility: Raft-based consensus service; manages ring topology; detects node failures via heartbeats

components.name: Client Library
components.responsibility: Consistent hashing client; routes requests to correct node; handles retries and failover

components.name: Replication Manager
components.responsibility: Async replication from primary to replicas; sync replication for write-through mode

components.name: Eviction Engine
components.responsibility: SLRU + Clock algorithm; TTL expiration; memory pressure monitoring

components.name: Monitoring Service
components.responsibility: Collects hit/miss rates, latency percentiles, memory usage; exposes

components.name: Admin API
components.responsibility: Node management (add/remove/rebalance); cache flush; stats queries

questions.title: 2. Node Failure and Recovery
questions.content:
The cluster coordinator sends heartbeats to all nodes every 1 second. If a node misses 3 consecutive
heartbeats, it's marked as failed. One of its replicas is promoted to primary (the replica with the most up-to-date
data, determined by replication offset). The coordinator updates the ring topology and gossips the change to all
clients. Clients that receive a 'node unavailable' error retry with the new topology.

questions.title: 3. Hot Key Problem
questions.content:
A hot key (e.g., a viral post's metadata) can overwhelm a single cache node. Mitigation strategies: (1) Local
client-side cache: each application server caches the top 1000 hot keys in a local LRU cache (L1 cache),
reducing remote cache hits. (2) Key replication: the cache client detects hot keys (>1000 req/sec) and
replicates them to 3 random nodes, distributing reads. (3) Request coalescing: multiple concurrent requests for
the same key are collapsed into a single backend request.

questions.title: 4. Memory Management
questions.content:
Each cache node pre-allocates a fixed memory pool (e.g., 64GB) at startup to avoid OS memory fragmentation.
The memory pool is divided into slab classes (8B, 16B, 32B, ... 1MB) similar to Memcached's slab allocator.
Each key-value pair is stored in the smallest slab class that fits it. This eliminates fragmentation and makes
allocation O(1).

questions.title: 5. Client-Side Consistent Hashing
questions.content:
The client library maintains a local copy of the ring topology. On startup, it fetches the topology from the
coordinator. It subscribes to topology change events via a long-poll or WebSocket connection. When a topology
change is received, the client updates its local ring atomically. This means routing decisions are made entirely
client-side with zero network overhead, achieving sub-microsecond routing latency.

keyFlows: ① Client Lib hashes key → routes to correct Node (consistent hash ring)
② Node serves GET from SLRU cache; evicts LRU on memory pressure
③ SET: Primary writes → async replicates to 2 Replicas
④ Coordinator (Raft) detects node failure → promotes Replica to Primary
⑤ Topology changes gossipped to all Client Libs via long-poll

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study the Raft consensus algorithm in depth: leader election, log replication, and how it
handles network partitions — this is the foundation of distributed coordination.
Expert Note: Learn about slab allocation (Memcached's approach): how pre-allocated memory pools
eliminate fragmentation, the trade-off between slab class granularity and internal fragmentation.
Expert Note: Understand the thundering herd problem in caches: how cache stampedes occur when a
popular key expires, and mitigation strategies like probabilistic early expiration and mutex-based request
coalescing.
Expert Note: Study gossip protocols: how they propagate cluster state changes in O(log N) rounds, their
eventual consistency guarantees, and how they compare to centralized coordination.
Expert Note: Learn about the TinyLFU admission policy used in Caffeine (Java) and how it combines a
frequency sketch with SLRU to achieve near-optimal hit rates.
